item.title: REXONA SUVA MARATHON 2026 | From marshal to runner
item.description: Every race has the power to change lives, not just for those who cross the finish line, but also for the volunteers and marshals who make it happen. For Payal Kritiya, Team Leader Marketing at Motibhai, the Suva Marathon 2025 inspired her journey from event organiser to first-time participant. Having worked behind the scenes supporting previous races, she witnessed first-hand how sporting events b
item.content:encoded:
Every race has the power to change lives, not just for those who cross the finish line, but also for the volunteers and marshals who make it happen.

For Payal Kritiya, Team Leader Marketing at Motibhai, the Suva Marathon 2025 inspired her journey from event organiser to first-time participant. Having worked behind the scenes supporting previous races, she witnessed first-hand how sporting events bring communities together and encourage healthier lifestyles.

This year, she’s swapping her supporting role for running shoes and a bib.

“I wanted to experience the Rexona Suva Marathon from a participant’s perspective,” says Payal.

“Running the event is a personal challenge and reflects my commitment to the healthy and active lifestyle the marathon promotes.”

Like many working women, balancing work and personal commitments isn’t always easy. But Payal believes even short exercise sessions make a difference, helping her manage stress and stay energised. Her message to anyone thinking about joining is simple: “Believe in yourself and take the first step. You don’t need to be an experienced runner, just start at your own pace and enjoy the journey.”

Whether you’re a volunteer, a walker, or a runner, you never know where your first race might lead. Sometimes, all it takes is showing up.

1. What was your job in previous running events? And which running events did you do? In previous running events, I worked behind the scenes as part of the event coordination and marketing team. My responsibilities included planning event logistics, coordinating with sponsors and stakeholders, overseeing promotional activities, managing participant communications, and ensuring that operations ran smoothly from preparation through to race day. I have been involved in several community and corporate sporting events, each providing valuable experience in delivering successful events.

2. How did it happen for you to get involved in the first place? My passion for sports, community engagement, and event management naturally led me to become involved in running events. Through my role in marketing and customer engagement, I saw how sporting events bring people together while promoting healthier lifestyles. It was an opportunity to combine my professional skills with something I genuinely enjoy.

3. Was it your first time involved in a running event? No, it was not my first experience. Over the years, I have had the opportunity to support and coordinate several sporting events. Also, a passionate Netball Player.

4. On the day of the race, how did you find it? Did it meet your expectations? Race days are always exciting and rewarding. Although there are many moving parts that require constant attention, seeing participants cross the finish line with a sense of accomplishment makes all the hard work worthwhile. The event met and, in many ways, exceeded my expectations because of the positive feedback from participants, the strong community support, and the teamwork demonstrated by everyone involved. Suva Marathon 2026

5. Why do you want to run this year? Why this event? This year, I want to experience the Suva Marathon from a participant’s perspective while continuing to support it as an event coordinator. Running the event represents a personal challenge and reflects my commitment to living the healthy and active lifestyle that the marathon promotes. As the coordinator for the Rexona Suva Marathon, I believe it is important to lead by example and encourage others to embrace fitness.

6. Did you set a training plan to prepare for the run? Are you exercising more leading up to the event? Not really – honestly netball and hockey practice keeps me on my toes, so does work.

7. How do you think you will feel at the end of the race? At this point I cannot confirm however – I believe I will feel an overwhelming sense of pride, accomplishment, and gratitude. Completing the race will not only represent a personal achievement but will also be rewarding because I will witness the success of an event that so many people have worked hard to deliver. Crossing the finish line alongside hundreds of participants will be a memorable moment.

8. What advice would you give to other women or athletes who are thinking of signing up for this race? My advice is to believe in yourself and simply take the first step. You do not need to be an experienced runner to participate. Start at your own pace, remain consistent with your training, and focus on enjoying the journey rather than comparing yourself to others. Events like the Rexona Suva Marathon are about personal achievement, community, and celebrating a healthy lifestyle. Every participant, whether walking or running, has something to be proud of. General

9. What do you like about Suva Marathon events? What I appreciate most about the Rexona Suva Marathon is its ability to unite people from all walks of life. It brings together families, friends, corporate teams, experienced athletes, and first-time participants in a positive and encouraging environment. The event promotes health, resilience, teamwork, and community spirit while showcasing the vibrant atmosphere of Suva.

10. What are the challenges that you face in making time to exercise? Like many working women, balancing professional responsibilities with personal commitments can make it challenging to maintain a consistent exercise routine. There are days when work schedules are demanding, but I have learned that prioritising my health is essential. Even short exercise sessions make a significant difference, and staying active helps me manage stress, maintain energy, and perform better both personally and professionally.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Ratu kicks Cuvu in
item.description: THE unforgettable drama at Lawaqa Park last Saturday saw Cuvu College under-18 fly-half Taniela Ratu’s unwavering faith guide him to slot a crucial conversion that secured a dramatic 27-26 victory over Natabua High School (NHS). His kick not only sealed the win but also ensured Cuvu remained top of the Western Zone’s U18 division, while bringing a painful end to NHS’s campaign.
item.content:encoded:
THE unforgettable drama at Lawaqa Park last Saturday saw Cuvu College under-18 fly-half Taniela Ratu’s unwavering faith guide him to slot a crucial conversion that secured a dramatic 27-26 victory over Natabua High School (NHS).

His kick not only sealed the win but also ensured Cuvu remained top of the Western Zone’s U18 division, while bringing a painful end to NHS’s campaign.

NHS needed a bonus-point win to secure their place in the national playoffs, but in the final moments, it was Ratu’s calmness and steadfast belief that saw him rise above the pressure.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Historic home final inspires Lomani
item.description: THERE are no words to describe what it means for the McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women captain Kolora Lomani to lead her side into the first-ever home Super Rugby Women’s grand final as they prepare to face the New South Wales Waratahs Women at Four R Stadium in Ba on Saturday. As she will captain the Drua Women in her third grand final, the occasion carries extra significance after playing the previou
item.content:encoded:
THERE are no words to describe what it means for the McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women captain Kolora Lomani to lead her side into the first-ever home Super Rugby Women’s grand final as they prepare to face the New South Wales Waratahs Women at Four R Stadium in Ba on Saturday.

As she will captain the Drua Women in her third grand final, the occasion carries extra significance after playing the previous two title deciders away from home.

“It’ll be very special. We played the last two finals away from home, and to be able to secure a home final is something no words can explain. Especially for us pioneer girls who have been here since 2022, to make history by playing a grand final here in Fiji is something we’ll never forget in our rugby career,” Lomani said.

The Drua enter the title decider seeking revenge after opening their 2026 campaign with a defeat 29-42 to the Waratahs.

However, Lomani believes Saturday’s contest will bear little resemblance to that Round One loss.

“We obviously lost our first game against the Waratahs, but this week will be a whole different level of rugby because it’s a grand final,” she said.

“We’ve prepared well for it, and we’re looking forward to it. We know it won’t be an easy game, but the girls have put in the hard work.”

“The coaches and management have reminded us how important and how tough this game will be.”

Lomani said the squad had enjoyed a productive build-up, completing two strong training sessions at the start of the week as they fine-tuned preparations for the biggest match in the club’s history.

“We’ve had two good sessions on Monday and Tuesday, and we’re really looking forward to the game on Saturday,” she said.

The captain is expecting a fierce contest but is confident the Drua have earned their place in the final after weeks of hard work.

“We’ve worked hard to be where we are and to earn this opportunity. It’s going to be a good game, and I think the crowd will really enjoy what the girls put out on Saturday.”

The occasion will also be an emotional one for Lomani personally, with her parents travelling to Fiji from Australia to watch her play for the first time in a home final.

After missing last week’s semi-final victory, her mother and father surprised her with the news they would be making the trip.

“I received a surprise call from Mum and Dad on Monday, and they told me they’ll be here for the final,” she said.

Lomani also issued a heartfelt call to supporters, urging fans to pack Four R Stadium and create an atmosphere worthy of the historic occasion.

“To all our friends, family and supporters, we hope you come out in numbers on Saturday,” she said.

Drua Women will take on Waratahs Women at 1.05pm.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: U18 ready for clash
item.description: THE Vodafone U18 Deans elimination rounds will be held next Saturday, with the top teams from the respective zones preparing for a crucial battle for a place in the quarterfinals. West Zone official Sekove Rasuka confirmed that the top two teams from each zone will host their elimination matches, including teams from the Southern, Eastern, Northern and Western zones.
item.content:encoded:
THE Vodafone U18 Deans elimination rounds will be held next Saturday, with the top teams from the respective zones preparing for a crucial battle for a place in the quarterfinals.

West Zone official Sekove Rasuka confirmed that the top two teams from each zone will host their elimination matches, including teams from the Southern, Eastern, Northern and Western zones.

The top four teams from each zone have qualified for the elimination stage, but only the winning teams from next week’s matches will progress to the quarterfinals.

In the West Zone, Cuvu College and Ba Provincial Freebird will host their matches, while Ratu Navula College and Ra High School will travel north to face the Northern Zone’s top two teams.

Rasuka said the zone is currently working on confirming venues for the top two matches, with most grounds unavailable due to Skipper Cup commitments.

“Right now, we are trying to look for the venue to host our number one and two games. All venues are booked because of Skipper Cup, but we will look at one venue which will be favourable to both sides in terms of travelling and expenses,” Rasuka said.

The elimination rounds will determine the teams that continue their journey towards the national Vodafone U18 Deans title.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Drua W unchanged
item.description: THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women will head into the biggest match in the club’s history with an unchanged line-up against NSW Waratahs at Four R Stadium in Ba on Saturday. Head coach Mike Legge retained the same Match-Day 23 that powered the side into the 2026 Super Rugby Women’s Grand Final.
item.content:encoded:
THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women will head into the biggest match in the club’s history with an unchanged line-up against NSW Waratahs at Four R Stadium in Ba on Saturday.

Head coach Mike Legge retained the same Match-Day 23 that powered the side into the 2026 Super Rugby Women’s Grand Final.

Captain Kolora Lomani, who starts at halfback alongside flyhalf Varanisese Qoro in a settled halves combination that has guided the Drua throughout the finals campaign. Veteran prop Karalaini Naisewa is set to make her 31st appearance for the club, extending her record as the most-capped Drua Women’s player. She starts at loosehead prop alongside her elder sister Keleni Marawa and tighthead prop Tiana Robanakadavu in an experienced front row. The second-row features Mereoni Nakesa starting at locks partnering Carletta Yee.

The back row remains unchanged with Nunia Daunimoala at blindside flanker, Salaseini Railumu on the openside and powerful No.8 Sulita Waisega completing a formidable loose forward trio. Aqela Raitubu starts on the left wing while prolific finisher Atelaite Buna takes her place on the right.

The flying Josivini Naihamu starts at inside centre while Merewairita Neivoha on the outside and Litiana Vueti at fullback.

Legge has also maintained continuity on the bench, with seasoned captain Bitila Tawake providing cover at hooker alongside front-row replacements Zipporah Sorokacika and Keri Lawavou.

Karavaki Lutumaibau and Alfreda Fisher will add muscle to the forward pack, while Evivi Senikarivi, Repeka Tove and Verenaisi Ditavutu provide backline versatility and impact in the closing stages.

In the injury cloud are fly-half Imeri Nai and utility back Ivamere Rokowati. With no changes to the match-day squad, the Drua will rely on the combinations, confidence and momentum built during their semi-final triumph as they seek to create history by claiming their first ever Super Rugby Women’s title in front of their home supporters in Ba.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Yasawa thrashes Taveuni, progress to semis
item.description: THE Yasawa women’s team delivered a commanding performance to thrash Taveuni 67-15 in their Royal Tea Ranadi Cup quarter-final clash at Buchurst Park One, Suva, yesterday. The western side dominated all aspects of the game, solidifying their status as strong contenders for this season’s title.
item.content:encoded:
THE Yasawa women’s team delivered a commanding performance to thrash Taveuni 67-15 in their Royal Tea Ranadi Cup quarter-final clash at Buchurst Park One, Suva, yesterday.

The western side dominated all aspects of the game, solidifying their status as strong contenders for this season’s title.

Head coach Tuliana Ragadi, who also plays as the team’s tight head prop, said their hard work in preparation had paid off, keeping them on course for their ultimate goal – winning the competition.

“We had prepared really well for this quarter-finals match, and we thank Taveuni for the great game they gave us today. Today (yesterday) we came and we did what we needed to do,” Ragadi said.

She added, “For that we give thanks to the Lord Almighty for his strength and guidance during the match, and now another task awaits us as we look forward to the semi-finals.”

Despite the impressive scoreline, Ragadi acknowledged there was room for improvement ahead of the semi-finals.

“We did a number of mistakes today, when we get to Lautoka tomorrow (today) this will be something we need to discuss and work on if we are to book our finals spot this season,” she said.

Ragadi also took a moment to recognise the unwavering support of those behind the scenes, who have been the backbone of the team from the start of the season.

In other Ranadi quarter-final results, Cakaudrove thrashed Tavua 62-15, Navosa defeated Northland 41-24, and Northern Bulls crushed Serua 71-7.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Teams book semi spots
item.description: CAKAUDROVE have secured their place in the Vanua Challenge semi-finals after a hard-fought 19-11 victory over Tavua in their quarter-final clash yesterday at the HFC Bank Stadium, Laucala Bay, Suva. Early dominance in the first half proved decisive as Tavua was forced to play catch-up rugby throughout the second half.
item.content:encoded:
CAKAUDROVE have secured their place in the Vanua Challenge semi-finals after a hard-fought 19-11 victory over Tavua in their quarter-final clash yesterday at the HFC Bank Stadium, Laucala Bay, Suva.

Early dominance in the first half proved decisive as Tavua was forced to play catch-up rugby throughout the second half.

The northerners showed resilience and discipline to hold off the Tavua onslaught until the final whistle.

Captain Aquino Savenaca described the achievement as a proud moment, successfully leading his provincial team to the semi-finals.

“This is a really happy and proud moment for me, it’s really an honour to represent my Vanua. This is just my second year with the team and I’m so glad we have made it to the semi-finals this year,” Savenaca said.

The win is particularly special for a team that missed out on the top four in last year’s competition.

For most players, it was their first time playing a quarter-final at the national stadium, marking a significant milestone in their young rugby careers.

“There are a lot of improvements we need to make before the semi-finals. For most of the players, this has been a very big exposure for them,” he explained.

“Most of them, this is their first quarter-final in the national stadium where the game is probably broadcasted. It really showed on the field, and we need to improve on that before the semis.”

In other results, Vatukoula edged Macuata 43-32, Ra defeated Ovalau 34-28, while Northland defeated Namosi 23-21.

In the Vanua Challenge U20 quarter-finals, Macuata thrashed Vatukoula 40-17, Coastland edged Namosi 27-12, Cakaudrove beat Tavua 23-13, and Northland defeated Ovalau 24-8.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji’s global handball debut team receive blessings
item.description: A NEW chapter for Fiji handball began at Nasoqo House yesterday morning as the Under-18 Women’s Handball Team presented their i-Tatau ahead of their maiden appearance at the International Handball Federation (IHF) Women’s Youth World Championship in, Romania. The ceremony, attended by Minister for Housing and Local Government Maciu Nalumisa, gave the young players an opportunity to seek blessings
item.content:encoded:
A NEW chapter for Fiji handball began at Nasoqo House yesterday morning as the Under-18 Women’s Handball Team presented their i-Tatau ahead of their maiden appearance at the International Handball Federation (IHF) Women’s Youth World Championship in, Romania.

The ceremony, attended by Minister for Housing and Local Government Maciu Nalumisa, gave the young players an opportunity to seek blessings and acknowledge the support behind their journey to the world stage.

“You are not only representing yourselves, but your family, village and nation as a whole,” Nalumisa said.

“You are ambassadors and will be representing Fiji to the world stage.”

For the 12-memeber squad, the occasion marked the beginning of a challenge they have spent month preparing for. They will become the first Pacific Island Nation to compete at the IHF Women’s Youth World Championship, a milestone that highlights the growth of handball in the region.

Head of Delegation Lusi Rokoura said the team has remained focused on preparing the players physically and mentally for the challenge ahead.

“They’ve been working really hard, they’ve been eager and can’t wait to take on the games in Romania,” Rokoura said.

Rokoura said the focus is not only results but also on gaining valuable experience that face shape the future of the sport locally.

“We’re advising them to enjoy the game, to be themselves and to take as much out of this competition and this opportunity.”

The squad began their travels yesterday afternoon to Hong Kong first for several days to acclimatise before continuing to Romania, where they will face Croatia, France and Egypt in Group A.

Having qualified through last year’s Oceania Youth Championships in the cook Islands, the team now hopes their appearance will inspire more young athletes to take up handball in Fiji.

The tournament runs from July 29 to August 9, with Fiji entering the competition not only to gain international experience but to build a stronger pathway for the future of the sport.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
